pattern: The lcp matrix forces an equivalence-class structure on the positions (two positions must hold the same letter exactly when their mutual lcp value is positive), so the lexicographically smallest word is built by greedily assigning the next unused letter to each still-open class, then the guess must be checked by rebuilding its true lcp matrix from scratch.
hints: Wherever lcp[i][j] is greater than zero, positions i and j must hold the same letter; wherever it is zero, they must differ. That already tells you which positions belong together.

To get the lexicographically smallest word, scan positions left to right; the first time you reach a position without a letter yet, give it the next unused letter and immediately assign that same letter to every other position sharing a positive lcp with it.

A guess is not automatically correct: rebuild the lcp matrix that your constructed word would actually produce (working from the last position backward) and compare it, cell by cell, against the one you were given; any mismatch, or running out of letters, means the answer is the empty string.

edge_inputs.description: two positions forced to the same letter
three positions all forced to distinct letters
the diagonal value is inconsistent with the remaining length at that position
the matrix is not symmetric, which no real string could produce
a plausible-looking matrix whose implied chain of equal letters is inconsistent

stress.description: lcp matrices generated from real random strings, so a valid answer always exists
